subject: Duplicate invoice sent to Meridian for August 2024 billing cycle ($28,000 duplicate)
description: Meridian Financial's accounts payable team received two separate invoices for the August 2024 billing cycle, both for the full base platform fee of $28,000 — the amount specified in SOW Section 5.1 for the Enterprise tier. The two invoices were issued under document numbers INV-MER-20240901-A and INV-MER-20240901-B and arrived in Meridian's AP queue within 14 minutes of each other on the morning of 2024-09-01. The AP contact who identified the duplication was Ryan Kim, Senior Manager of Accounts Payable at Meridian, who flagged it via email to Elena Martinez at 11:18 ET on 2024-09-01. Ryan Kim reported that Meridian's AP automation system would have queued both invoices for payment under its standard NET-30 processing workflow had the duplication not been detected manually during the daily AP review. The estimated risk window before automatic payment would have been triggered was approximately 48 hours. The incident was immediately escalated to P0 by Elena Martinez because billing integrity constitutes a financial risk even before money actually moves. Root cause investigation by Priyanka Shah confirmed that a Stripe webhook delivering the BillingPeriodClosed event for Meridian's August billing cycle was replayed by Stripe after a transient 502 timeout on Orion's ingestion endpoint. Orion's billing event processor was keying idempotency on the locally generated invoice number rather than on the inbound Stripe event ID. Because the timestamp had advanced by 14 minutes between the original event and the replay, the processor generated a new invoice number (INV-MER-20240901-B) and treated the replayed event as a new billing event, producing a second $28,000 invoice. The duplicate invoice was voided by contacting Ryan Kim directly at 13:05 ET on 2024-09-01, within 1 hour and 47 minutes of the initial customer report — well within the 4-hour P0 resolution SLA. A hotfix correcting the idempotency keying logic was deployed to production on 2024-09-02. A written root cause analysis document was delivered to Ryan Kim and Sarah Chen on 2024-09-04.
business_impact: Financial impact was zero since the duplicate was voided before payment. Reputational impact: Meridian's AP team is now auditing all Orion invoices going back 12 months, adding operational overhead to their finance team. The incident has increased scrutiny on Orion's billing processes from Ryan Kim's team at a time when the platform is already under executive attention due to the login latency issue (TICK-4521). Sarah Chen has been briefed. No service credits are owed because resolution was within the 4-hour P0 SLA.
preventive_measures: Idempotency keying changed from generated invoice number to inbound stripe_event_id across all billing event processors — the inbound event ID is stable across retries, so replayed events are now recognized and deduplicated

Alert added for more than one BillingPeriodClosed event per tenant per 24-hour window, routing to the on-call billing engineer with a PagerDuty page at P1 severity so future duplicates are caught before they produce invoices
Integration tests updated to cover the webhook replay scenario with a synthetic 502 timeout from the Stripe edge, ensuring the idempotency fix is regression-tested on every deploy
lessons_learned: Idempotency must always key on the inbound event identifier provided by the upstream system, not on identifiers generated by the receiving system — generated IDs advance with the clock, making them unique even for replayed events and defeating the purpose of idempotency checks

Webhook retry behavior from payment providers must be explicitly covered in integration test suites, not just in unit tests — a unit test that mocks the webhook payload cannot exercise the replay scenario because it does not simulate the provider's retry logic

P0 billing issues should trigger immediate automated suspension of duplicate payment processing until a human reviewer confirms that the event is legitimate — the current system continued processing because it did not recognize the replay as a duplicate

The AP contact at key enterprise accounts should be identified and stored in the customer record so that billing corrections can be routed directly to the right person without escalation delay — in this case Elena Martinez had Ryan Kim's contact from the onboarding process, but that path should be automated
stripe_webhook_context: Stripe's webhook retry policy sends up to three retries with exponential backoff when the receiving endpoint returns a 5xx response or times out. Orion's ingestion endpoint returned a 502 at 11:02:14 ET because the internal billing processor was temporarily unavailable during a routine configuration reload. Stripe's first retry fired at 11:16:08 ET, 14 minutes later. This is consistent with Stripe's documented minimum retry interval of 5 minutes for the first retry. Orion's endpoint was available again by then and returned 201 Created on the retry. Both the original request at 11:02 and the retry at 11:16 produced BillingPeriodClosed events in Orion's billing event log. Because the idempotency check used the generated invoice number (which is timestamp-derived and therefore different for each invocation), both events were treated as distinct billing triggers and both produced invoices.

comments.author: Elena Martinez
comments.body: Opening as P0 per SOW Section 2.2 — billing integrity is a financial impact risk even if the dollar amount has not yet moved. Ryan Kim at Meridian flagged this via email at 11:18 ET. Two invoices received in Meridian's AP queue: INV-MER-20240901-A and INV-MER-20240901-B, both for $28,000. Ryan estimates their AP automation would queue both for payment within 48 hours if not corrected. I need eyes on the billing event log immediately. Paging Priyanka Shah.

comments.author: Priyanka Shah
comments.body: Confirmed in our billing event log: two separate BillingPeriodClosed events for tenant_meridian_prod, timestamps 2024-09-01T11:02:14-04:00 and 2024-09-01T11:16:08-04:00. Both events triggered invoice generation. The 14-minute gap is consistent with Stripe's first-retry window after a failed webhook delivery. Checking our endpoint availability logs for 11:02 ET to confirm whether we returned a 5xx at that time.

comments.author: Priyanka Shah
comments.body: Root cause confirmed. Our ingestion endpoint returned a 502 Bad Gateway at 11:02:07 ET because the internal billing processor was mid-reload during a scheduled configuration update. The 502 caused Stripe to retry the webhook at 11:16:08 ET. Our billing processor was available by then and returned 201 Created on the retry. However, our idempotency check keyed on the invoice number that the processor generated locally — since 14 minutes had elapsed, the timestamp component of the invoice number was different, producing INV-MER-20240901-B as a distinct invoice. The processor correctly interpreted this as a new event and generated a second invoice. The bug is in the idempotency strategy: we should have been keying on the Stripe event ID provided in the webhook payload, which is stable across retries. I am preparing a hotfix now. The correct key is stripe_event_id from the webhook body, which Stripe guarantees is identical across all retry attempts for the same event.

comments.author: David Park
comments.body: Called Ryan Kim at Meridian directly to void INV-MER-20240901-B. Ryan confirmed receipt of the void at 13:05 ET and has removed the duplicate from their AP queue. Meridian's AP automation will not process INV-MER-20240901-B. Ryan is understandably concerned and has asked for a written root cause analysis within 5 business days. He mentioned that this incident has prompted his team to initiate a manual audit of all Orion invoices going back 12 months. I've reassured him that this is the first billing error in Meridian's 14-month account history and offered to share the invoice history directly from our billing system to support their audit. Elapsed time from customer report (11:18 ET) to void confirmed (13:05 ET): 1 hour 47 minutes. This is well within the 4-hour P0 resolution SLA. I'm marking the immediate incident as resolved but keeping the ticket open until the hotfix is deployed and the RCA is delivered.

comments.author: Elena Martinez
comments.body: Briefed Sarah Chen on the incident. She acknowledged that the financial impact was zero and that resolution was fast. However, she noted that this incident, combined with the ongoing login latency issue (TICK-4521), has increased scrutiny on Orion's operational reliability from her finance team and CFO. She specifically asked that the RCA document explain not just what happened but what systemic controls will prevent recurrence. I've relayed this to Priyanka. The RCA needs to cover: timeline, root cause, immediate fix, preventive measures, and a statement that we have reviewed billing history for other enterprise tenants and found no similar duplicates. Please also include confirmation of the updated integration test coverage.

comments.author: Priyanka Shah
comments.body: Hotfix deployed to production via a dedicated hotfix window 2024-09-02 14:00-14:30 UTC. The billing event processor now extracts stripe_event_id from the inbound webhook payload and uses it as the primary idempotency key before any processing occurs. If the same stripe_event_id is received more than once, the processor returns the original 201 response immediately without regenerating an invoice. I also added a secondary guard: if the processor receives two BillingPeriodClosed events for the same tenant within a 24-hour window, it logs a WARN-level alert and pages the on-call billing engineer before generating any invoice. This two-layer approach means a duplicate will be blocked at the idempotency layer and surfaced to a human even if the idempotency layer somehow fails. Unit tests for the idempotency fix are merged. The integration test covering the webhook replay scenario with a synthetic 502 timeout is staged for merge by end of day — it simulates Stripe's retry by sending the same event payload twice with a 14-minute gap in the synthetic timestamp, and verifies that only one invoice is generated.

comments.author: Priyanka Shah
comments.body: Integration test merged and passing in CI. The test scenario: (1) send a BillingPeriodClosed webhook to the billing processor with stripe_event_id=evt_test_001 and confirm one invoice is generated; (2) send the identical webhook payload with the same stripe_event_id again with a 14-minute offset in the received_at timestamp; (3) confirm the second invocation returns 201 immediately without generating a second invoice; (4) confirm the idempotency log records the deduplication event. All four assertions pass. I also ran the integration test against the prior version of the processor (before the hotfix) to confirm it would have failed — it correctly generates two invoices on the pre-fix version, validating that the test catches the regression. This test will run on every deploy to production from now on. Coverage report updated.

comments.author: David Park
comments.body: RCA document delivered to Ryan Kim and CC'd to Sarah Chen per their request. Document covers: full timeline from 11:02 ET to 13:05 ET on 2024-09-01; detailed root cause explanation (Stripe webhook replay plus incorrect idempotency keying); hotfix details (stripe_event_id keying, deployed 2024-09-02); preventive measures (dual-layer idempotency, on-call alert for duplicate billing events, integration test coverage); statement that we reviewed billing event logs for all 47 enterprise tenants and found no other instances of duplicate BillingPeriodClosed events in the past 90 days. Ryan Kim acknowledged receipt by email at 11:45 ET on 2024-09-04 and confirmed the RCA meets Meridian's requirements for their internal audit file. He noted that the 12-month invoice audit their team initiated found no other discrepancies. This ticket will remain open one additional business day for any follow-up, then close.

comments.author: Priyanka Shah
comments.body: No further follow-up questions from Ryan Kim or Sarah Chen. All preventive measures are in production: (1) idempotency keying changed to inbound stripe_event_id — deployed 2024-09-02; (2) alert configured for more than one BillingPeriodClosed event per tenant per 24 hours — deployed 2024-09-02; (3) integration tests for the webhook replay scenario merged and passing in CI — merged 2024-09-03. Ryan Kim's 12-month audit found no other billing discrepancies. Closing as resolved. Financial impact was zero. Resolution time was 1 hour 47 minutes against a 4-hour SLA. No service credits are owed per SOW Section 2.3.

resolution: Duplicate invoice INV-MER-20240901-B voided within 1h 47m of customer report. Root cause: billing event processor was keying idempotency on generated invoice number rather than inbound stripe_event_id, causing a Stripe webhook replay triggered by a transient 502 to generate a second invoice. Hotfix deployed 2024-09-02 changes idempotency keying to stripe_event_id. Preventive alert added for duplicate BillingPeriodClosed events per tenant per 24 hours. Integration tests updated to cover the webhook replay scenario with a synthetic 502 timeout. RCA delivered to Ryan Kim and Sarah Chen on 2024-09-04. No service credits owed — resolution within SLA.
